CLARITY.justification: The paper has a logical overall structure (Introduction → Problem Setting → Method → Experiments → Related Work → Conclusions) and the core notation and equations (e.g. Equations (1)–(6), Proposition 1, Algorithm 1) are defined so the main idea is understandable. However, the manuscript contains many presentation problems that hinder readability: duplicated and garbled blocks of text (multiple repeated paragraphs in Sections 1–3), placeholder/boilerplate fragments (“Blah-blah”, ACM copy text) and a corrupted Figure 1 area, and some typographical errors (e.g. “Dimentionality”, “succesfully”). Figures/tables are referenced but not cleanly presented, and a few formatting inconsistencies reduce clarity despite the otherwise coherent organization.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clear and novel reframing of SGNS as a two-step procedure (search for low-rank X, then factorize X) and directly optimizes the original SGNS objective over the manifold of rank-d matrices (Proposition 1, Sec. 2.2), which contrasts with prior SVD-over-SPPMI surrogate approaches (Levy & Goldberg) and standard SGD on W,C (Intro, Sec. 6.1). It applies an existing Riemannian retraction method (projector-splitting; Lubich & Oseledets 2014) to this problem (Sec. 3.1.2), yielding empirical improvements—so the contribution is a nontrivial and well-differentiated application of known optimization machinery to SGNS rather than inventing new math. Because the core mathematical tools are established, the novelty is notable and applied rather than foundational, hence a 4 rather than 5.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing improved SGNS objective and higher Spearman correlations (Sec. 5, Tables referenced) and the reformulation of SGNS as optimization over X is standard and correctly given (Sec. 2.2, Proposition 1). Mathematical steps are correct: the loss depends only on inner products, the gradient formula in Sec. 3.2 is algebraically sound, and the projector-splitting retraction / Algorithm 1 follows established Riemannian literature (Lubich & Oseledets). Major concerns are experimental: only enwik9 is used, important baseline/training hyperparameters and statistical significance are not reported, and the use of SVD-SPPMI initialization (Sec. 5) may bias gains; these are minor issues that do not invalidate the technical contribution.
